title: To provide a private right of action against the maker of any component of a ghost gun, and any person who facilitated a sale of the ghost gun, for injury or death resulting from the use of the ghost gun.
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill lets people who are hurt by ghost guns sue the companies that made the gun parts. It also lets families of people killed by ghost guns sue these companies. Ghost guns are guns that do not have the special numbers that regular guns must have by law. The bill also allows lawsuits against anyone who helped sell these ghost guns. This gives victims and families a way to take legal action against those responsible for making or selling these unmarked weapons.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1 Private Right of Action
sections.text: This section allows certain people to sue in federal court when someone is hurt or killed by a ghost gun. A ghost gun is a firearm that does not have a unique serial number stamped on it by a licensed gun maker or importer as required by federal law. Ghost guns also include any combination of parts that are designed to be put together to make a firearm or to convert another device into a firearm. People who can sue include the person who was injured, or if someone was killed, their family members. The state and local government where the injury or death happened can also sue. They can sue the company that made any part of the ghost gun or any person who helped sell the ghost gun. The lawsuit must be filed in the federal court in the area where the injury or death occurred. If the person suing wins their case, the court can award them money for damages including additional damages that resulted from the injury or death. There are two situations where the person being sued can defend themselves. First, if the injury or death happened because someone was defending themselves or another person against what they reasonably believed was an immediate threat to someone who was not breaking the law. Second, if the injury or death happened because a police officer was responding to what they reasonably believed was an immediate threat to public safety.
